coredata.dc:title: One-step facile hydrothermal synthesis of heteroatom-doped porous graphene reduced comparatively by different reductants for high performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Rong
Lai, Liuqin
Su, Siyu
Dai, Huimin
Cui, Yifan
Zhu, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Taking advantage of hydrothermal methods to reduce graphene oxide (GO) is a convenient way and different heteroatoms on graphene or reduced graphene oxide (RGO) are vitally significant to its performance for application in supercapacitors. Accordingly, our work here is to implement a facile one-step hydrothermal reduction approach to obtain diversely doped graphene using various reductants of HI, Na2S2O3, NaBH4, N2H4·H2O, CS(NH2)2, by which I-doped, S-doped, B-doped, N-doped, and N, S dual-doped RGOs (represented as I-RGO, S-RGO, B-RGO, N-RGO, N/S-RGO, respectively) were fabricated. Through electrochemical characterization, it is presented that their electrochemical capacitance properties are ranked approximately in the order of N/S-RGO > N-RGO > S-RGO ≥ B-RGO > I-RGO, whereas I-RGO is in possession of a wider potential window than the others. Further structural and chemical analyses indicate that the O content within the RGOs is a primary effect on their performance, followed by the assistances from disparate heteroatom dopants existed in the RGOs and the porous construction inside graphene. As a result, a high specific capacitance of 263.3 F g-1 is obtained in N/S-RGO at the current density of 1 A g-1.

head.ce:title: One-step facile hydrothermal synthesis of heteroatom-doped porous graphene reduced comparatively by different reductants for high performance supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610064, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Taking advantage of hydrothermal methods to reduce graphene oxide (GO) is a convenient way and different heteroatoms on graphene or reduced graphene oxide (RGO) are vitally significant to its performance for application in supercapacitors. Accordingly, our work here is to implement a facile one-step hydrothermal reduction approach to obtain diversely doped graphene using various reductants of HI, Na2S2O3, NaBH4, N2H4·H2O, CS(NH2)2, by which I-doped, S-doped, B-doped, N-doped, and N, S dual-doped RGOs (represented as I-RGO, S-RGO, B-RGO, N-RGO, N/S-RGO, respectively) were fabricated. Through electrochemical characterization, it is presented that their electrochemical capacitance properties are ranked approximately in the order of N/S-RGO > N-RGO > S-RGO ≥ B-RGO > I-RGO, whereas I-RGO is in possession of a wider potential window than the others. Further structural and chemical analyses indicate that the O content within the RGOs is a primary effect on their performance, followed by the assistances from disparate heteroatom dopants existed in the RGOs and the porous construction inside graphene. As a result, a high specific capacitance of 263.3 F g-1 is obtained in N/S-RGO at the current density of 1 A g-1.

ce:section.ce:para: Presently, flexible supercapacitors (FSCs) as novel and high efficient energy storage devices are promising candidates in the area of energy storage owing to their higher power densities than batteries with sufficient specific/volumetric energy for portable, flexible electronics, as well as the advantages of lightweight, high safety and eco-friendly for natural environment, and thus are widely used in wearable storage device applications, such as electronic-skin [1], power packs, roll-up displays, electronic paper, biosensors, micro-robots, wearable/implantable medical equipments [2], printed electronics (PEs) [3], mobile phones [4] and computers [5]. To this end, graphene, a one-atom layer of graphite possessing compelling properties with a large theoretical specific surface area (2675 m2 g-1) [6], has attracted considerable attention as a powerful material for flexible supercapacitors [7], due to its ultra-light weight, high electrical and thermal conductivity, strong mechanical properties, superior optical properties, large specific surface area, stable chemical properties and high flexibility [8]. Therefore, in recent years, there have been much efforts mainly focused on preparing graphene for excellent performances so as to improve the application, which is normally acquired by graphene oxide (GO). Meanwhile, it is known to us that there are also many researches on the way to reduce GO, for instance, by using solvent reduction, hydrothermal reduction, photoreduction, electrochemical deposition, CVD, etc [9]. Generally speaking, the chemical reduction of GO into graphene is believed to be the most efficient and low-cost method. However, hydrothermal method is considered as the most commonly adopted means in various reports in view of its high efficiency and convenience, and also, the obtained graphene exhibited good performances [10]. An approach via hydrothermal reduction to prepare the self-assembled 3D graphene oxide was reported, which exhibited high gravimetric capacitances (up to 220 F g-1) with maintaining >80% of its initial capacitance after 10000 cycles for the fabricated binder-free flexible supercapacitor [11].

An et al acquired fluorinated graphene hydrogels (FGHs) synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal process and applied in supercapacitors, which exhibited a high specific capacitance of 227 F g-1, a considerable capacitance retention of 94% (155 F g-1) obtained by cycling at a current density of 2 A g-1 for 2000 cycles, and a great power density as high as 50.05 kW kg-1 at the current density of 50 A g-1 [12]. A mild hydrothermal reaction of GO dispersion was employed for supercapacitors, where a specific capacitance of 62.3 F g-1 at the scan rate of 5 mV s-1 and 95% of the initial capacitance after 10000 cycles were achieved; moreover, capacitance measurements during folding/unfolding cycles demonstrated a superior stability of only about 27% decrease after 100 cycles folding-unfolding [13]. Li et al reported that the hydrothermally activated graphene fiber fabrics (HAGFFs) showed a maximum areal capacitance of 1060 m F cm−2 with a good rate capability and a long cycle life (50000 charge/discharge cycles) [14]. Luo et al synthesized graphene quantum dots/three-dimensional graphene (GQD/3DG) composites via a one-step hydrothermal treatment of GQDs and GO, exhibiting a specific capacitance of 242 F g−1 and an outstanding cycling stability of the capacitive retention of 93% after 10000 cycles [15].

According to the relevant reports previously, different doped heteroatoms (N, S, P, O, etc) were introduced into graphene by different deoxidizers, which can significantly improve the properties of graphene and considerably broaden their applications in diversified domains

[16,17]. Therefore, in the process of reducing GO into reduced graphene oxide (RGO), the selection and use of reducing agents are multiple. The most commonly used reducing agents are hydrazine hydrate, ammonia water and NaBH4. To the best of our knowledge, however, there are few reports on use of HI, Na2S2O3 and CS(NH2)2 as related reducing agents [18,19].

In this work, we report a one-step facile hydrothermal route to synthesize 3D heteroatom-doped graphene by using several different reductants. And then the chemically obtained RGO was fabricated as binder-free electrodes with carbon cloth as base for FSCs. Thus, this work is primarily focused on the effects and comparison of different reducing agents on the graphene obtained by the hydrothermal reduction of GO, which could generate I-doped, B-doped, N-doped, S-doped, and N,S-dual doped graphene. Then, through characterization and performance analysis of the RGOs, we have demonstrated how these several varied element dopants affect the electrochemical properties of 3D porous heteroatom-doped graphene dissimilarly. As a consequence, this work is of guiding significance for an effective method to prepare graphene with improved electrochemical performances and widened perspectives, and therefore paves the way for the application of graphene for FSCs.

Chemicals used in this work include graphite powder, potassium permanganate (KMnO4), concentrate sulphuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrochloric acid (HCl), hydroiodic acid (HI), sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), potassium hydroxide (KOH), hydrazinium hydroxide (N2H4·H2O), thiourea (CS(NH2)2) and deionized water (H2O). All these reagents were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory Co., Ltd except for graphite powder from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Note that the carbon cloth (WOS1009) was purchased from Cetech Co., Ltd. Just as summarized in Table 1 , all the chemical reagents in our experiment were analytically pure, and then were used as received without further purification.
Graphene oxide (GO) was fabricated by a modified Hummers’ method via the oxidation reaction proposed before. [20] Details of the modified procedure are described as follows.

Firstly, flake graphite powder (4 g) was added into the mixture of KMnO4 (16 g) and concentrated H2SO4 (100 mL, 98%), stirring in an ice water bath for 2 h. Next, the suspension was heated at a temperature of 40 °C for 40 min in a water bath. Following that, 260 mL of deionized water was slowly poured into the solution with a stabilized temperature of around 40 °C. Successively, the obtained suspension was maintained at 90 °C for 30 min to react continually. Thereafter, 10 mL of 30% H2O2 was added to the solution above, and subsequently, it was filtrated and washed using 5% HCl acid and deionized water, which was followed by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 15 min several times to acquire pure graphene oxide. Then, the GO was achieved by ultrasonication of the dispersion in an ultrasonic bath. Finally, the homogeneous GO aqueous dispersion was obtained for later use.

RGO aerogel was prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method. For this purpose, the concentration of the GO gel solution was adjusted to 1 mg/ml by diluting with H2O to get aqueous GO. Afterwards, the reductants, 0.5 ml HI, 0.5 g Na2S2O3, 0.5 g NaBH4 (the reagent, NaBH4, was immerged into the solution of 5 mol/L KOH, 5 ml for use), 0.5 ml N2H4·H2O and 0.5 g CS(NH2)2 were respectively added into a 50 ml aqueous GO solution of 1 mg/ml concentration, which was loaded in the 100 ml Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Then, ultrasound treatment was performed for acquisition of the mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the RGO hydrogel was filtrated and fully washed by deionized water, followed by a freeze-drying treatment for 48 h to obtain the RGO aerogel. The five RGO samples reduced by HI, Na2S2O3, NaBH4, N2H4·H2O and CS(NH2)2 were named as I-RGO, S-RGO, B-RGO, N-RGO and N/S-RGO, respectively.

The morphology characterizations of the as-prepared samples were acquired via scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-4800, Hitachi, Ltd., Chiyoda-ku, Japan, operated at 30 kV). The phase structure was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD; DX-1000, Dandong Fangyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., Dandong, China) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm). Raman spectroscopy was collected on a Raman spectrometer with 532 nm excitation wavelength (HORIBA JobinYvon XploRA, France). Chemical state analysis was carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI-5000C ESCA System, USA), for which an Al source, conducted at 300 W with pass energy of 93.9 eV, was utilized.

The electrodes were fabricated for electrochemical measurements. Firstly, the active materials prepared were mixed in a small amount of ethyl alcohol solvent, followed by ultrasonication to form homogeneous slurry. After that, the slurry was coated on carbon cloth uniformly and then dried at 60 °C in a drying oven. The mass loading of active material on each electrode was calculated according to the so-called difference method using electronic analytical balance to weigh the carbon cloth before and after loaded with active materials, of which the weight for active materials on each electrode was controlled to be ca. 1 mg. The electrochemical performance analysis was performed through an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, CH Instruments, Inc., Austin, TX, USA) using a standard three-electrode cell configuration.

The value of specific capacitance (C, F g−1) was evaluated from charge-discharge (GCD) curves according to the equation given below:
(1)
C m
=
I× Δ t
m× Δ V
where C m (F g-1), I (A), Δ V
(V), Δ t
(s) and m (g) are the specific capacitance, discharge current, the potential window, discharge time and the mass of the active material of the work electrode on the carbon cloth substrate determined by the weight differences, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization for structure and morphology
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the strong reducibility of these deoxidizer HI, Na2S2O3, NaBH4, N2H4·H2O and CS(NH2)2, it is meaningful to utilize them for the reduction of graphene by a mild hydrothermal process, and according to the reduction of graphene by these reductants, there are some impact facts found in this study so as to obtain graphene with anticipant good properties for application in varied fields. The hydrothermal reduction process for the fabrication of RGOs (I-RGO–N/S-RGO) is shown in Fig. 1 . At the beginning, the GO nanosheets were dispersed ultrasonically, followed by the addition of different reductants into the dispersions. Afterwards, the different RGOs were acquired via a hydrothermal procedure.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared RGO samples (I-RGO–N/S-RGO) are displayed in Fig. 2 , which manifests two broad peaks at 24.5° and 43.6°, corresponding to (002) and (100) reflections, respectively [21], indicating that GO prepared in this work was successfully reduced to graphene and peeled off into disordered and incompact structure by several different reductants via hydrothermal reduction generally.

As shown in Fig. 3 and Table 2 , the Raman spectra depict two characteristic strong bands of D band and G band at around 1350 cm-1 and 1592 cm-1, respectively [19], implying a disordered crystal structure of the RGOs by combination with the aforementioned XRD results as well as weak bands of 2D band and 2 G band at around 2700 cm-1 and 2900 cm-1, respectively, for all the samples [22]. D band is related to the disordered structure and defects in the sp2 carbon network, while G band is attributed to the first order scattering of the E 2g stretching vibration mode observed for the sp2 carbon to explain the crystallinity of the materials [23]. 2D band is due to a second order dispersive Raman mode for the typical peak in graphitic carbon materials [24]. Therefore, this result is in line with the XRD results, exhibiting the structural information of RGOs. And also the value of I D/I G (the intensity ratio between D band and G band) is generally used to describe the disorder degree of graphene as well as the lattice defect for carbon-based materials [25,26]. According to the evaluated values for the RGOs, as displayed in Table 2 and Fig. 3, it is found that the I D/I G value of N/S-RGO is the highest, revealing a more defective and structurally disordered nature. It is also worth mentioning that the 2D band in N/S-RGO is more visible and intensive than the others, further proving the existence of more disordered crystal structures of N/S-RGO [27]. Especially, S-RGO has the smallest I D/I G value, while the values of others are all larger than 1, which is caused by introduction of defects in the period of pore formation [28]. This is consistent with the following phenomenon from SEM observations, in which the richness of porosity in S-RGO is less than the others.

To understand the microstructure and morphology of the RGOs, their SEM images are illustrated in Fig. 4 , which demonstrate that all the RGO materials have a loose and porous 3D structure with only slight agglomeration. It means that the hollow structures can supply varied abundant surface area between the electrode and the electrolyte, thereby providing additional space for the gathering of a large number of electrolyte ions to promote the ion diffusion during charging and discharging process [29]. At the same time, the porous microstructure is capable of offering abundant reaction sites for faradaic pseudocapacitance on graphene. Note that the pore size of the RGO materials is mainly of mesopore and macropore range.

To explore the specific surface area (SSA) and the pore distribution, Brunauer-Emmett-Teller technique was implemented. The relevant data are presented in Fig. 5 and Table 3 . Fig. 5(a) shows the corresponding nitrogen absorption/desorption isotherm curves. The isotherm of all the RGO materials manifests a type-IV curve with a hysteresis loop, which is the characteristic of mesoporous microstructure. Meanwhile, the pore diameter distributions of the RGOs is investigated by desorption isotherms utilizing Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method, as shown in Fig. 5(b). The SSA and average pore size of the RGOs are therefore summarized in Table 3, revealing that such porous microstructure can offer more pathways and help to accelerate the diffusion of ions and electrons in the electrolyte to some extent [30,31]. It should be pointed out that S-RGO possesses fewer pores than the others, which is likely to have an adverse impact on its property, as will be elucidated below.

XPS was investigated to explore the elemental composition and chemical states of the RGOs, the result of which is given in Fig. 6 and Table 4 . The XPS full spectrum for all the samples is revealed in Fig. 6(a). It is necessary to note that the reason why a little of N element is comprised in samples I-RGO, S-RGO and B-RGO is that during the reaction, there was N2 from air, which might join the reaction and then N could be doped into the RGO materials. Meanwhile, since the concentrated H2SO4 was used for preparation of GO, a small amount of S element also appears in I-RGO, B-RGO and N-RGO. But these low-level N and S amounts might have only minor influence on the properties. The XPS results show the significant existence of I, S, B, N, dual N and S in their respective RGO samples, confirming that the I-RGO, S-RGO, B-RGO, N-RGO, N/S-RGO are doped with I, S, B, N, dual N and S, respectively, thus denoting the formation of heteroatom-doped graphene.

Every RGO sample has its corresponding high-resolution XPS spectrum for different kinds of elements. As shown in subFigs. 6 (b)∼(f), the C1s spectrum of RGOs can all be dividied into C-C/C = C, C-O, C = O and O–C = O, π-π at ∼284, ∼285 or 286, ∼287, ∼289, ∼291 eV, respectively. Deservedly, C1s spectrum of all the RGOs is equiped with unique carbonyl functional groups, with C-I (∼285 eV) in I-RGO, C-S (∼285 eV) in S-RGO, C-B (∼283 eV) in B-RGO, C-N (∼286 eV) in N-RGO, and C-N/-S(∼285 eV) in N/S-RGO [32–36]. Meantime, the O1s spectra consist of four main peaks appearing at ∼530, ∼531, ∼532, ∼533 eV corresponding respectively to O-S, O = C, O-C = O, O-C, which agrees with the C1s [32,37,38]. As far as the individual RGO is concerned, first of all, there display two primary peaks Id5/2 and Id3/2 at about 619.1 and 631.7 eV, respectively, which attestes that a number of I atoms were doped into I-RGO [39]. And the two representive peaks for I3d of I-RGO were splited into four peaks emerged in Fig. 6(b), which are imputed to I3¯and I5¯ at around 618.6/630.3 and 620.3/631.8 eV, and it is a stable status in the polyiodides form of iodine atoms as dopant to graphene. [40,41] As for S2p spectrum of S-RGO and N/S-RGO, S2p1/2 of ∼165.2 eV and S2p3/2 of ∼163.9 eV belong to two adjacent conterminous peaks. Four splited peaks are assigned to thiophene-S (C-S-C) owing to their spin-orbit coupling and different oxidized sulfur states of C-SOx-C (x = 2, 3, 4) at ∼163.8, ∼165.1, ∼168.1 and ∼169.3 eV, respectively [32,42–44]. The B1s XPS in B-RGO is resolved into two peaks, which are allocated to B-C and B-O at ∼189.5 and ∼192.3 eV approximately. In addition, the deconvoluted O1s spectra of B-RGO diaplay B-O, B-C-O bands at binding energies ∼533.5, ∼532.6 eV, respectively [45,46]. The N1s core level spectra of N-RGO include pyridinc nitrogen (N6) at ∼398.1 eV, pyrrolic nitrogen (N5) at ∼400.3 eV, and quaternary/graphitic nitrogen (NQ) at ∼401.4 eV, pyridine N-oxide (NX) at 402.3 eV as well as chemisorbed nitrogen at 404.8 eV, roughly [47–49]. Nevertheless, the other RGOs do the same as N-RGO, with ∼398.7, ∼400.3, ∼401.1, ∼402.3 eV respectively for N6, N5, NQ, NX, merely except for amides/amines or lactams (NC) at ∼399.6 eV instead of chemisorbed nitrogen [50–52]. Hence, the analysis above interpretes the accomplishment of disparate heteroatoms incorporated in the RGOs seperately.

To further evaluate the electrochemical performance of the RGO materials, we tested their electrochemical properties with a three-electrode system. In Fig. 7 (a), cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves are detected in a voltage range of -0.5 to 0.5 V at the scan rate of 5 mV s-1, which show approximately rectangle shapes due to the predominant double-layer capacitor behavior [53]. On the one hand, for S-RGO, N-RGO and N/S-RGO, heteroatoms such as N, O and S dopants in graphene, as observed in the XPS spectra, could possibly result in an enhanced surface wettability on carbon-based materials with inducing a pseudocapacitive behavior, like pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and the sulfur dopant species, such as thiophenic-like sulfur and sulfone, thereby boosting the performance of supercapacitors prominently and explaining the appearance of peak for pseudocapacitive behavior for all the samples [16]. On the other hand, for B-RGO and I-RGO, the faradaic contribution from redox reactions of the active functional groups on the surface of the electrodes leads to the coexistence of electrical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and pseudocapacitance [23], which reflects the presence of oxygen-containing functionalities, as verified by XPS measurements. In detail, the typical interconversion of the quinone/hydroquinone groups is supported by the C = O and C-OH binding forces, which could generate apparent pseudocapacitance [54,55]. And for I-RGO, there is an additional peak source from I3¯and I5¯, as mentioned previously in XPS.

The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) curves in Fig. 7(b), which were measured at the current density of 1 A g-1, resemble a triangle, typical for double-layer capacitors and thus in accordance with the CV results. The specific capacitance of I-RGO, S-RGO, B-RGO, N-RGO and N/S-RGO is 168.33, 179.4, 183, 205 and 263.3 F g-1, respectively, demonstrating excellent electrochemical responses in the RGOs. Furthermore, it is notable that the potential windows cover both positive and negative regions, which broadens the utilization of carbonaceous material for practical energy storage devices [56].

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) curves are recorded in Fig. 7(c) with high frequency data magnified in the inset. It is deduced from the figure that the value of R s (serial resistance) for RGOs is 0.924, 0.793, 0.792, 0.781 and 1.062 Ω, respectively, signifying that the RGO electrodes have relatively low intrinsic resistances and good conductivities when compared with those similar materials reported previously [26,57,58]. Notwithstanding the biggest R s value for N/S-RGO, which might be likely ascribed to the more generated defects than the other RGOs during the hydrothermal reaction, it has an unconspicuous adverse impact on the electrochemical property owing to the fact that porous microstructure plays a crucial role in facilitating the faster charge transfer and ion migration pathways across interconnected open mesoporous and macroporous 3D structures into the electrolyte, [59] and the N, S dopants develop N and S-containing functional groups for the enhancement of electrochemical performance [44]. Besides, negligible semicircles with small diameters at the high frequency region denote very low charge transfer resistances (R ct). Interestingly, however, there are two semicircles that emerged in the EIS curves, corresponding to the confirmed pseudocapacitive characteristics [60]. The one at relatively lower frequency can be ascribed to redox reactions with functional groups taking place on the materials surface, while the other from high to medium frequency, signifying the parallel combination of charge-transfer resistance and double layer capacitance as the semicircle at high frequency, is related to the surface properties of the electrode corresponding to the faradic charge-transfer resistance [61,62]. Moreover, almost vertical lines obtained at low frequencies for all the samples declare the ideal dominance of double-layer supercapacitors and high efficient electron transfer and ease of ion transport at the electrode/electrolyte interface.

The rate capability of the RGOs under different current densities is summarized in Fig. 7(d), indicating that the specific capacitances of all the samples show roughly same downward tendency with the raise of current density but with different decreasing degrees. Nevertheless, even at a high current density of 20 A g−1, 78% (130.8 F g-1), 78% (140.6 F g-1), 77% (140.4 F g-1), 80% (163.2 F g-1) and 70% (184.2 F g-1) of the initial capacitances are still maintained, which claims a prominent rate capability performance. Possibly, this superior property stems from porous microstructure, stimulating the shift of ions/charges traversing mesopores and macropores.

The cycling life durability of the electrode materials is a significant factor for assessment of electrochemical properties in supercapacitors. The capacitance retention presented in Fig. 7(e) is 80%, 88%, 89%, 94% and 96% for the five different RGOs, respectively, after 1000 cycles of charging and discharging at the current density of 1 A g-1, confirming their promising applications in high-performance energy storage system.

Furthermore, it is demonstrated in Fig. 8 that I-RGO could be analyzed in a wider voltage window from -0.6∼0.6 V. Hence, we can see that although I-RGO is poorer than the other graphene materials on the property by electrochemical analysis, it should be pointed out that owing to its widened potential window, this material is a selectable good candidate for more application areas than supercapacitors, for example, the field for reversible electrochemical hydrogen storage [63].

On the whole, I-doped graphene (I-RGO) is poorest in the aspect of electrochemical property, while the N,S dual-doped one (N/S-RGO) is the best among all the RGOs, followed sequentially by the N-doped one (N-RGO) and the B-doped one (B-RGO), which is nearly identical to the S-doped one (S-RGO). As for the manifestation, we suppose that it is mainly related to the oxygen content. The less O atom% is, the more complete the reduction reaction gets [24]. The proportion of O is associated with the size molecules/atoms of these reductants. Under the circumstance of high temperature in hydrothermal reaction, the H2O molecule moved fast because of its light and little nature; as GO was involved in H2O, H2O carried GO together to shift quickly as well. Unfortunately, the heavy weight of HI hindered the speed for movement, resulting in the delayed and insufficient reduction. In the same way, due to the molecular dimension order for HI > Na2S2O3> NaBH4> CS(NH2)2> N2H4, the O percentage of RGOs is ranked by HI > Na2S2O3> NaBH4> CS(NH2)2> N2H4, which conforms to the information in Table 2. As a result, from Table 4 about XPS, I-RGO involves the maximum amount of O. This means that I-RGO is imperfectly reduced by HI, leading to a weak behavior of EDLC stemmed from graphene, albeit the possession of pseudocapacitance from I and O function groups, which is just a moderate promotion because the I proportion is little. A lower O% in B-RGO than in I-RGO implies that B-RGO was better reduced, and thus the electrochemical property of B-RGO is higher than that of I-RGO, which is also beneficial from its more porous skeleton framework than I-RGO. But fewer B heteroatoms and more O atoms in B-RGO lead to a weaker feature than N-RGO an N/S-RGO on performance. S-RGO gained the lowest S BET and least pore distribution among all the RGOs from BET and SEM measurements, which forges the poorer property due to more O scale in S-RGO than in B-RGO, N-RGO and N/S-RGO. With regard to S-RGO and B-RGO, the percentage of O atom of S-RGO is slightly bigger than that of B-RGO; notwithstanding, the S element was brought in S-RGO, supporting a rescue of the property, which verifies that S as heteroatom dopant is indeed helpful in enhancing the performance. This point would get more certification on the comparison about S-RGO, N-RGO and N/S-RGO. Similarly, according to the comparison of performance, S-RGO has more O content than N-RGO and N/S-RGO. However, the quantity of O in N-RGO is less than in N/S-RGO; moreover, the N content of N-RGO is much more preferable than N/S-RGO, and S-RGO is stronger than N/S-RGO in the possession of S element. Besides, in terms of morphology, N-RGO consists of the richest porosity than the others, traced by N/S-RGO. But N/S-RGO prevails over all the RGOs for property, and N-RGO is superior to S-RGO. The cause is probably that N/S-RGO is profited from the coexistence of N and S heteroatoms, and the synergistic contribution from dual N and S dopants leads to an advanced property. Consequently, it may be inferred that the influence from N, S on property surpasses that of B, I, and the mutual effect of N and S coexistence is a key point.

It is worth mentioning that the lattice defects induced by N and S heteroatoms are capable of offering the reaction sites and enhancing the electron conductivity as well. And the defect density in graphene may be increased more effectively by N, S dual doping than by N or S sole doping. Therefore, the S content in S-RGO is less than the N content in N-RGO, leading to the electrochemical property of S-RGO inferior to that of N-RGO. The total content of heteroatoms for N and S in N/S-RGO is higher than the S content in S-RGO, while lower than the N content in N-RGO; however, due to the synergistic effect from N and S co-doping, the performance of N/S-RGO is the best among all the RGOs.

It was reported previously that doped graphene could be well applied for energy storage devices like batteries and supercapacitors. Nevertheless, the effects from diverse heteroatoms as dopants might exhibit similarities and differences in some aspects. There is a general likelihood that most of heteroatoms doped graphene materials have exerted a valid impact on enhancing property for supercapacitors or batteries. For instance, with the help of alternate conversion of iodine in the form of I3-/I5- during charge/discharge process, the I-doped graphene (I-RGO) is able to be applied in symmetrical or asymmetrical supercapacitors as anode. It can also be used as an anode material in lithium-ion batteries, in which the reactions of iodine happen on the hydroxyl group of the graphene substrate, reinforcing the electrical conductivity [40,64,65].

For B-doped graphene (B-RGO), normally as negative electrode, could be similarly fabricated into supercapacitors and Li-batteries. The incorporation of boron functionalities for boron-oxygen or boron-carbon linking to the graphene surface or edge may serve as active reaction sites for exciting electrochemical procedure, and the B atoms that were doped into graphene may activate the π system, which is beneficial for electron motion [46,66].

If applied in Li/Na-ion batteries or supercapacitors as anode, the S-doped graphene (S-RGO), could get improved in performance. This is probably attributed to the introduction of S heteroatoms, with which O and C atoms in graphene may prompt oxygen- and carbon- containing sulfuric groups, thereby elevating the electrochemical activity, and the electron-rich thiophene ring related to S heteroatom could furnish a highly polarized surface that may lead to an increment in dielectric constant of the electrolyte, thus inducing the enriched charge transfer [67,68].

Besides, what is possible is that the supercapacitors and Li-batteries with N-doped graphene (N-RGO) as anodes may strengthen their performance on electrochemical behavior by oxygen and carbon containing functional groups of nitrogen, and N doping may reinforce the electrical conductivity for faster charge transfer and then induce defect sites for more lithium storage [69]. Meanwhile, more favorable binding sites compared to carbon may be created by higher electronegativity of nitrogen for the promotion of performance [70]. Likewise, it is interpreted that the enhancement on property of N, S-dual doped graphene (N/S-RGO) for supercapacitors might stem from the incorporation of N and S by combination with C and O to shape functional groups together with the corporate synergistic effect of N, S contents by intensifying the electronic conductivity in Li-ion batteries and supercapacitors as anodes [71]. Furthermore, these heteroatoms doped graphene could be useful in some other storage systems, like Li-S batteries, especially for stopping sulfur motion in Li-S batteries; similar doping concepts as described in this manuscript are used often as a second current collector or layer on the cathodic side of the separator [72–74].

From the above results, we can see that N/S-RGO shows the optimal electrochemical response among all, and thus N, S co-doped graphene has more preferable performance than N-doped (N-RGO) and S-doped (S-RGO) ones, which however prevail over the B-doped (B-RGO) and I-doped (I-RGO) counterparts. These results reveal that the coexistence of N and S heteroatoms is the best choice and the contribution from N element dopant in RGO is better than that from S, B and I, while the S-doped (S-RGO) effect on property is roughly equal to the impact from B (B-RGO).

The reasons are mainly attributed to the three points listed below. The first one is that the O content within RGOs, which is connected to the molecule size of reductants applied during the reduction process, principally determines the electrochemical properties. Secondly, the various heteroatom dopants in graphene exert a positive effect on performances, thereby increasing the capacitance. Thirdly, the porous skeleton also acts as a factor that enables the improvement of electrochemical performance. These heteroatoms doped RGOs are qualified for applications in Li/Na-ion batteries or supercapacitors as anodes and also qualified for application in Li-S batteries.

Rong Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Liuqin Lai: Formal analysis, Validation. Siyu Su: Methodology. Huimin Dai: Data curation. Yifan Cui: Data curation. Xiaohong Zhu: Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
